coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly nanocatalysts for hydrogen generation via sodium borohydride hydrolysis and enhanced fuel cell applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ekinci, Arzu
Şahin, Ömer
Turgut, Sevnur
Baytar, Orhan
coredata.dc:description: Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) hydrolysis is a promising method for sustainable hydrogen production, but its efficiency depends on high-performance and eco-friendly catalysts. This study introduces a green hydrothermal synthesis using tobacco stem extract to fabricate Co–Sb nanoparticles, reducing toxic chemical usage compared to conventional methods. Catalytic tests confirm their high efficiency in NaBH4 hydrolysis, with hydrogen generation increasing at higher temperatures and NaOH/NaBH4 concentrations. Structural analyses (XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM) reveal an amorphous, porous morphology with spherical particles (∼20–30 nm). Co–Sb nanoparticles achieve a hydrogen generation rate of 5618 mlmin−1g−1, surpassing Co nanoparticles (5360 mlmin−1g−1), with lower activation energy (30.9 kJ mol−1 vs. 35.5 kJ mol−1), highlighting Co–Sb's superior catalytic efficiency—additionally, 1 wt% Sb doping enhances hydrogen production and improves PEM fuel cell performance, reaching a peak power density of 137.3 mW/cm2. However, reusability tests of the Co–Sb catalyst revealed a decline in the hydrogen generation rate, indicating catalyst deactivation due to oxidation on active surfaces and the accumulation of by-products. Furthermore, excessive Sb doping was found to block active sites on the catalyst surface, thereby reducing its efficiency. This study underscores the viability of green-synthesized Co–Sb nanoparticles for hydrogen generation and fuel cell applications, offering a sustainable alternative to energy technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Extract
Nanoparticles
Co–Sb
Hydrolysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD analysis of Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles.
SEM image of nanoparticles (a) Co; (b) Co–Sb.
TEM image of nanoparticles (a) Co; (b) Co–Sb.
EDX analysis of Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles.
FT-IR analysis of Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles.
Hydrogen generation curve of the hydrolysis of NaBH4 solution for Sb-supported cobalt catalysts at different mole fractions.
The hydrolysis of different concentrations of NaOH in the presence of 25 mg of Co–Sb catalyst at a solution temperature of 30 °C.
The hydrolysis of different concentrations of NaBH4 in the presence of 25 mg of Co–Sb catalyst at a solution temperature of 30 °C.

(a)The impact of temperature on the catalytic efficacy of Co–Sb catalyst; (b) The impact of temperature on the catalytic efficacy of Co catalyst; (c) HGR on the reaction temperature of Co and Co–Sb catalysts; (d) Arrhenius plots for the catalytic activity of Co and Co–Sb catalysts.
The reusability of the Co–Sb catalyst for five cycles (Conditions: 25 mg, 30 °C, 2.5 wt% NaBH4).
(a) Polarization curve of Co–Sb NPs catalyst, (b) Current density change versus time.
HGR and Ea values of as-obtained Co–Sb catalyst and other reported Co-based catalysts toward the hydrolysis of alkaline NaBH4 solution.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: Hydrogen Production and Transportation (Pfeifer) published in International Journal of Hydrogen Energy.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly nanocatalysts for hydrogen generation via sodium borohydride hydrolysis and enhanced fuel cell applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) hydrolysis is a promising method for sustainable hydrogen production, but its efficiency depends on high-performance and eco-friendly catalysts. This study introduces a green hydrothermal synthesis using tobacco stem extract to fabricate Co–Sb nanoparticles, reducing toxic chemical usage compared to conventional methods. Catalytic tests confirm their high efficiency in NaBH4 hydrolysis, with hydrogen generation increasing at higher temperatures and NaOH/NaBH4 concentrations. Structural analyses (XRD, FT-IR, SEM, TEM) reveal an amorphous, porous morphology with spherical particles (∼20–30 nm). Co–Sb nanoparticles achieve a hydrogen generation rate of 5618 mlmin−1g−1, surpassing Co nanoparticles (5360 mlmin−1g−1), with lower activation energy (30.9 kJ mol−1 vs. 35.5 kJ mol−1), highlighting Co–Sb's superior catalytic efficiency—additionally, 1 wt% Sb doping enhances hydrogen production and improves PEM fuel cell performance, reaching a peak power density of 137.3 mW/cm2. However, reusability tests of the Co–Sb catalyst revealed a decline in the hydrogen generation rate, indicating catalyst deactivation due to oxidation on active surfaces and the accumulation of by-products. Furthermore, excessive Sb doping was found to block active sites on the catalyst surface, thereby reducing its efficiency. This study underscores the viability of green-synthesized Co–Sb nanoparticles for hydrogen generation and fuel cell applications, offering a sustainable alternative to energy technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, green synthesis has emerged as a sustainable and environmentally friendly approach for nanoparticle production. This method minimizes or eliminates the use of toxic substances while avoiding the need for high temperatures, pressure, or expensive chemicals. Various biological sources, such as microorganisms and plant extracts, can serve as precursors in green synthesis [1–5]. Recent studies have demonstrated the successful synthesis of a wide range of nanoparticles using this approach. For instance, Sivasakthi et al. [6] produced carbon nanotubes by heating coconut shells at 450 °C, while Baytar et al. [7] synthesized NiO nanoparticles from watermelon seed shells and investigated their catalytic activity in sodium borohydride (NaBH4) hydrolysis. Similarly, Şahin et al. [8] synthesized NiO nanoparticles using banana peel extract and evaluated their efficiency in methylene blue degradation and NaBH4 hydrolysis. Several other biological precursors have been reported for nanoparticle synthesis, including Rheum ribes shell [9,10], Ocimum sanctum leaves [11], Eleusine coracana extracts [12], corn stalk bark [13] lemon peel waste [14], and prawn shells [15] has been reported. As fossil fuel reserves decrease and environmental pollution increases, hydrogen, a renewable energy source, has attracted significant global attention as a promising clean and renewable energy carrier [16,17]. Hydrogen possesses broad applications, from transportation to electricity generation [18]. However, the efficient production, transportation, and storage of hydrogen remain key challenges that must be addressed for its widespread adoption. Consequently, researchers have focused on developing clean and safe hydrogen production techniques [19–21]. Among various hydrogen production methods, the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 has emerged as a promising approach due to its high hydrogen storage capacity (10.8 wt%), low molecular weight, non-toxic nature, stability under alkaline conditions, and recyclability [22]. However, spontaneous NaBH4 hydrolysis is slow and inefficient, necessitating the use of catalysts to enhance the reaction rate [23]. Various catalysts, including noble metals [24] and metal alloys [25,26], have been explored for this purpose. Although noble metal catalysts exhibit excellent catalytic efficiency, their high cost and limited availability restrict their widespread application. As a result, research has increasingly focused on non-noble metal catalysts, particularly cobalt-based catalysts such as pure cobalt [25], cobalt oxides [27], boron-based [28], and bimetallic Co [29,30], due to their cost-effectiveness, abundance, and high catalytic activity. Recent studies suggest that binary metal catalysts exhibit superior catalytic activity compared to single-metal catalysts due to synergistic interactions between multiple metal components, as seen in Co-M (M = Fe, Cu, Cr, W, Mo) systems [31,32]. The catalytic performance of Co–B catalysts can be further enhanced by modifying their surface electronic density through metal doping [33]. When Co nanoparticles are doped with a second metal, the resulting Co-M nanoparticles introduce an atomic diffusion barrier, thereby increasing catalytic efficiency [34]. The second metal also acts as an electron donor, enhancing the electron density of active metal atoms and facilitating reaction kinetics. Furthermore, even at low atomic concentrations, the second metal prevents nanoparticle agglomeration, thereby increasing the catalyst's surface area and improving its catalytic activity [35]. Several physical and chemical methods have been employed for catalyst synthesis, including thermal decomposition, high-temperature solution-phase synthesis, reduction, and hydrothermal microemulsion approaches [36]. However, the biosynthesis of nanoparticles has gained significant attention due to its eco-friendly and cost-effective advantages [8,37–39]. Recent studies have also demonstrated that incorporating elements such as Ni [32] and La–W [33] into Co–B catalysts significantly enhances catalytic activity through synergistic interactions. Additionally, the presence of heteroatoms (B, N, O, P, and S) in metal-free catalysts derived from green sources or acid modifications has been reported to improve hydrogen generation [1,34]. The biological extracts used in green synthesis directly influence the size, morphology, and surface chemistry of nanoparticles, playing a crucial role in determining catalyst performance. Tobacco stem extract was selected as a reducing agent and stabilizer due to its rich content of polyphenols, alkaloids, and other organic compounds. The hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups present in the extract modify the surface chemistry of the nanoparticles, enhancing their active surface area and facilitating stronger interactions between NaBH4 molecules and the catalyst. This modification not only improves the hydrogen production rate but also prevents nanoparticle aggregation, thereby enhancing the catalyst's long-term stability. Cobalt-based catalysts are widely utilized in NaBH4 hydrolysis; however, they are hindered by several limitations, including low active surface area, irregular morphology, and reduced stability due to oxidation and passivation. Furthermore, the synthesis of Co-based catalysts typically involves complex, costly processes and the use of toxic chemicals, raising both environmental and economic concerns. To address these challenges, the incorporation of antimony (Sb) into Co-based catalysts is explored as a strategy to enhance their catalytic performance. Antimony is an important element in electrocatalysis due to its high electron transfer capacity. According to literature and Pourbaix diagrams, antimony-based compounds exhibit significant stability against the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) under acidic conditions and in the presence of chlorine gas [40,41]. Additionally, Sb has been shown to enhance the stability of CoO by modifying its surface, which improves electro-oxidation performance [42,43]. Although there is a lack of research on the role of Sb in sodium borohydride hydrolysis, combining cobalt with Sb is expected to enhance catalytic efficiency by leveraging the synergistic properties of both elements.

In this work, tobacco stem extract is employed for the hydrothermal synthesis of Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles, offering a sustainable and cost-effective approach to address the limitations of Co-based catalysts. The synthesized nanoparticles were characterized using SEM, EDX, XRD, TEM, and FTIR techniques, and their catalytic performance in NaBH4 hydrolysis was systematically evaluated. This study represents the first comprehensive investigation of Sb-doped Co catalysts in sodium borohydride hydrolysis, demonstrating their potential for hydrogen production and fuel cell applications.

These chemicals were used in the study: Cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (Alfa Aesar); antimony (III) iodide, SbI3 (Sigma-Aldrich); sodium boron hydride, NaBH4 (Sigma-Aldrich); and sodium hydroxide, NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich). Tobacco stems used in the green synthesis method were obtained from Adıyaman city of Turkey.

Tobacco stem extract was prepared by hydrothermal method; 10 g of ground tobacco stem and 60 ml of pure water were placed in a 100 ml autoclave, then the autoclave was kept at 180 °C for 12 h and then cooled to room temperature. The tobacco stem extract was separated from the solid by filtration and kept in a closed container in the refrigerator to be used in the green synthesis of nanoparticles.

Co–Sb nanoparticles were synthesized from tobacco stem extract by green synthesis chemical reduction method. Green synthesis of Co–Sb nanoparticles: 2 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and sufficient SbI3 were dissolved in 50 ml tobacco extract at room temperature, then NaBH4, five times the total number of metal moles, was dissolved in 50 ml pure water and reduction was achieved by adding it drop by drop to the medium of the metal mixture. The synthesized Co–Sb nanoparticles were separated from the solution medium by filtration and dried in nitrogen at 120 °C for 3 h. Co–Sb nanoparticles were kept in a closed container for experiments and characterization.

The synthesized Co and Co–Sb NPs were characterized by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The morphological and structural properties of Co and Co–Sb NPs were determined by SEM (ZEISS-EVO 50 instrument, Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH, Germany) and XRD (Rigaku X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 154.059 p.m.), Japan) measurements, respectively. The morphology of Co and Co–Sb NPs were further examined by TEM using Tecnai-12 (Philips, Netherland). The basic element components of Co and Co–Sb NPs were determined by EDX (Bruker, Quantax ED-XS, Germany) analysis. The infrared spectrum was recorded with the Thermo Scientific Nicolet 95 IS10 FTIR spectrometer in the range of 4000–450 cm−1. Absorption spectra were recorded on the Lambda 365 UV-VIS spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the catalytic activity of catalysts for NaBH4 hydrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: The volume of hydrogen produced was measured using the water displacement method. To determine the optimum conditions in the experiment, different mole ratios of Sb, different NaBH4/NaOH concentrations, and different catalyst amounts were examined, and for each experiment, 25 mg of catalyst was placed in a flask. First, 10 ml of 2.5 wt% sodium borohydride solution and 2.5 wt% sodium hydroxide were left in a water bath at a constant temperature of 30 °C and then connected to a 1000 mL graduated tape measure. The volume of hydrogen produced was determined based on the displacement of water in the graduated tape measure. Once the optimum conditions were determined, the volume of hydrogen released was measured by reaction time at reaction temperatures of 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C. The hydrogen generation rate was calculated from the slope of the produced hydrogen volume graph plotted against time.

Kinetic studies on the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by the Co–Sb catalyst used the nth-order kinetic equation model. Based on the nth-order kinetics, the reaction rate per unit volume can be defined as [44].
(1)
−
r A
= −
d C A
d t
= k
C n
A
Where C represents the concentration, k represents the reaction rate constant, and r is the rate at which the reaction is occurring. The following equations may be found by the operations of separating and integrating:
(2)
−
∫
C A
0
C A
d C
A
C A
n
= k
∫ 0 t
d t
(3)
1
(
1 − n
)
(
1
C A
n − 1
−
1
C
A o
n − 1
)
= k t
(
n ≠ 1
)
Consequently, the slope of the line as a function of time from the graph of equation (3) can be used to determine the n-th order.

The Arrhenius plot was used to calculate the activation energy (Ea) for NaBH4 hydrolysis for the Co–Sb catalyst. The Arrhenius equation is given as follows:
(4)
k = A
exp
− E a
R T
Where k is the hydrogen generation rate (mL min−1g−1) (calculated from the linear part of the graph); A0, collision factor; Ea, activation energy; R, gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1); and T is absolute temperature (K). When plotting ln k against 1/T, it gives a straight line. The activation energy is calculated from the slope.

The crystal structure and crystallinity of the nanomaterial play a crucial role in determining its physical characteristics. Hence, the X-ray powder diffraction technique was used to investigate the crystallinity of cobalt nanoparticles and cobalt nanoparticles doped with antimony. This analytical method is often utilized to determine the phase, crystal structures, atomic spacing, and unit cell dimensions of crystalline materials [45]. The XRD analysis results of cobalt and antimony-doped cobalt nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 1 .

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed that Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles possess an amorphous structure, as no distinct Bragg reflections corresponding to Co metal were observed [46]. His indicates that the nanoparticles lack long-range crystalline order. Similarly, Garcia-Torres et al. reported that the XRD patterns of the Co@Ag core-shell nanoparticles they synthesized exhibited no detectable peaks, suggesting that the Bragg reflections of the metallic core were absent. They attributed this phenomenon to the formation of a core-shell structure, where the shell material may suppress the diffraction signals of the core [47]. The morphological characteristics of the synthesized nanoparticles were further analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), a powerful imaging technique that utilizes a focused electron beam to scan the sample and generate high-resolution images. When the electron beam interacts with the sample, it produces various signals, including secondary and backscattered electrons, which provide valuable insights into the sample's surface topography, texture, and microstructural features. Additionally, SEM enables the determination of particle size, shape, and distribution, offering critical information about the overall morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles. Furthermore, elemental composition and phase distribution can be assessed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), which is often integrated with SEM. This combined approach allows for a comprehensive structural and compositional characterization, contributing to a deeper understanding of the synthesized nanoparticles' properties and potential applications [48]. The SEM analysis results of cobalt and antimony-doped cobalt nanoparticles are given in Fig. 2 .

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that Co nanoparticles primarily consist of small, spherical structures with a homogeneous particle distribution and the presence of micropores on their surface (Fig. 2a). This observation aligns with findings in the literature, which report that Co nanoparticles generally exhibit a spherical morphology with particle sizes below 20 nm [49]. These results further validate the morphology of Co nanoparticles synthesized via green synthesis methods.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the incorporation of Sb into Co nanoparticles induces a partial transformation in their morphology, leading to the coexistence of both flat-layered and spherical structures. Furthermore, Co–Sb nanoparticles exhibit a pronounced tendency to agglomerate. Similar findings were reported by Puri et al., who demonstrated that Co–Sb nanoparticles synthesized via the solvothermal method also exhibited agglomeration, with particle sizes ranging from 20 to 30 nm [50]. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of the morphological characteristics and size distribution of the synthesized nanoparticles, TEM analysis was performed on both Co and Co–Sb samples (Fig. 3 ). This analysis provided high-resolution insights into their structural features, further elucidating the effects of Sb incorporation on nanoparticle morphology and aggregation behavior.

The morphology of the Co nanoparticles was observed to be predominantly spherical, with primary nanoparticles appearing as single crystals of nearly uniform size. Additionally, the directional connectivity of the particles was attributed to magnetic interactions between them. The particle size was determined to range between 20 and 30 nm, with most particles exhibiting a round morphology, although partial agglomeration was also evident, as shown in Fig. 3a. Fig. 3b illustrates that Co–Sb nanoparticles retain a spherical morphology; however, the introduction of Sb into the Co matrix significantly reduces the particle size. This reduction suggests that Sb doping influences nanoparticle growth dynamics, potentially altering nucleation and aggregation behavior during synthesis. Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was conducted to accurately determine the elemental composition of the synthesized nanoparticles. This analysis provided both qualitative and quantitative insights into the elemental distribution within the Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles synthesized via the green synthesis approach using tobacco stem extract. The corresponding results are presented in Fig. 4 and summarized in Table 1 .

The produced nanoparticles were subjected to EDX examination using energy standards ranging from 0 to 12 keV. The EDX spectrum in Fig. 3 demonstrates the prominent existence of cobalt nanoparticles. The 0.53 keV spectrum indicates the existence of Co Lα, whereas the 6.96 keV and 7.72 keV spectra also indicate the presence of Co Kα [51]. Moreover, strong peaks in the structure of Co–Sb nanoparticles indicate the presence of Co, and these results are similar to the peaks in the Co nanoparticle structure, and the peak at 3.40 keV energy in the spectrum shows the presence of Sb metal. Prior research has yielded comparable findings, and Moraes et al., in their investigation on the production of carbon nanotubes enhanced by antimony nanoparticles, reported that the energy peak corresponding to Sb metal in the EDX spectrum was measured at 3.40 keV [52]. The compositional data obtained from EDX analysis are in good agreement with the theoretically calculated values, and it can be seen in Table 1 that there is good compositional homogeneity among the nanoparticles. In the EDX spectra in Fig. 4, the typical spectrum at 0.30 keV is an indication that there is a carbon reflection peak, that it originates from tobacco extract, and that the nanoparticles are synthesized green sythesis. In addition, it is seen that Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles were synthesized without additives, that is, there is no any peak indicating impurity.

FT-IR spectroscopy is a non-destructive technique that fingerprints a sample with absorption peaks that match vibrational frequencies between the bonds of the material. Materials have different combinations of atoms and therefore result in different infrared spectra [53]. FT-IR analyses of Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles synthesized by the green synthesis method were carried out in the wave number range of 4000–450 cm−1, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 .

The spectrum in Fig. 5 has a prominent broad peak at a wave number of 3400 cm−1, which may be attributed to the existence of OH− in the molecular structure. Additionally, there are two distinct peaks at wave numbers 1550 cm−1 and 1256 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of CN and C–O bonds, respectively. The peak seen at a wave number of 1000 cm−1 is a result of the O–H bond present in the structure. These peaks are derived from tobacco extract because Co and Co–Sb nanoparticles were produced by the use of tobacco extract, confirming the effective accomplishment of green synthesis. The peak at wave number 545 cm−1 shows that the Co metal is octahedral coordinated in the structure as Co3+ [54].

In this study, the catalytic performance of Co catalyst prepared by green synthesis method in NaBH4 hydrolysis reaction was investigated in detail and the optimum Sb mole fraction was determined. Besides the optimum Sb doping level, the effect of different NaOH/NaBH4 concentrations, catalyst amounts and solution temperatures were systematically evaluated to determine the optimal reaction conditions. Moreover, multiple cycle tests were performed to study the stability and reusability of the catalyst. These comprehensive analyses have contributed to the identification of parameters that enhance the efficiency of the Co–Sb catalyst in hydrogen production and revealed the critical role of optimizing the synthesis conditions on the catalytic activity.

In Fig. 7, the NaBH4 concentration was maintained at a constant 2.5 wt%, while the catalyst amount was fixed at 25 mg, allowing for an investigation into the effect of varying sodium hydroxide concentrations (0 wt%, 1 wt%, 2.5 wt%, 5 wt%, and 7.5 wt%) on the hydrolysis process. In catalytic hydrolysis reactions, hydroxide ions play a dual role in influencing reaction kinetics. At lower hydroxide ion concentrations, negatively charged OH− ions adsorb onto the catalyst surface, generating electrostatic repulsion between catalyst particles. This repulsion is an ionic (Coulombic) interaction that occurs due to the presence of like charges on the catalyst surfaces, preventing particle aggregation. As a result, catalyst dispersion in the reaction solution improves, enhancing the contact between the catalyst and sodium borohydride, thereby facilitating hydrolysis [56]. However, when the hydroxide ion concentration exceeds a critical threshold, excessive adsorption of OH− ions can alter the surface charge distribution of the catalyst. This excessive surface charge may lead to the screening of active sites, reducing their accessibility for NaBH4 molecules. Additionally, high OH− concentrations can modify the reaction environment, potentially shifting equilibrium conditions and inhibiting the hydrolysis reaction. Thus, while moderate OH− concentrations enhance dispersion, an excessive amount can negatively impact catalytic activity.

Experimental findings revealed that increasing the NaOH concentration from 0 wt% to 2.5 wt% led to a significant rise in HGR. However, when the NaOH concentration was further increased to 5 wt%, a slight increase in HGR was observed initially, but a gradual decline followed. Specifically, the HGR was measured as 5618 mlmin−1g−1 for 2.5 wt% NaOH and 3440 mlmin−1g−1 for 5 wt% NaOH. While the initial increase suggests an enhancement in reaction kinetics due to improved catalyst dispersion, the subsequent decline is likely due to the excessive presence of OH− ions, which may have led to active site blockage or shifts in the reaction equilibrium, inhibiting further hydrolysis. To further investigate this trend, an additional experiment was conducted using 7.5 wt% NaOH, which resulted in a lower HGR compared to both 2.5 wt% and 5 wt% NaOH concentrations. This confirms that excessive hydroxide ions negatively affect the hydrolysis reaction, most likely due to increased site blocking and unfavorable equilibrium shifts. Based on these results, an optimal NaOH concentration of 2.5 wt% was selected for subsequent experiments to balance catalyst dispersion and reaction kinetics while avoiding inhibitory effects. Furthermore, the effect of varying sodium borohydride concentrations was investigated while keeping the NaOH concentration constant at 2.5 wt%, the catalyst amount at 25 mg, and the solution temperature at 30 °C. The resulting experimental data are presented as hydrolysis curves in Fig. 8 .

An increase in hydrogen production volume was observed as the NaBH4 concentration was raised from 2.5 wt% to 7.5 wt%. However, despite this increase in total hydrogen yield, the HGR decreased from 5618 mlmin−1g−1 to 3996 mlmin−1g−1. This decline in HGR can be attributed to the precipitation of borates in the aqueous solution once the sodium borohydride concentration surpasses a critical threshold. The formation of these precipitates obstructs the active sites on the catalyst surface, thereby inhibiting the interaction between reactants and catalysts and ultimately reducing the catalytic efficiency [1].

To further evaluate the effect of temperature on catalytic performance, the activity of both Co–Sb and Co catalysts was examined at different temperatures (30, 40, 50, and 60 °C). The corresponding graphs illustrating hydrogen generation volume as a function of time, as well as HGR variations with temperature, are presented in Fig. 9 a–b-c.

The hydrolysis of sodium borohydride at 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C was completed in approximately 5, 4, 2.5, and 1.5 min, respectively, for the Co–Sb catalyst (Fig. 9a). In contrast, for the Co catalyst, the reaction completion times at the same temperatures were 12, 6, 4, and 3 min (Fig. 9b). The superior catalytic activity of the Co–Sb catalyst compared to the Co catalyst can be attributed to the synergistic interaction between Co and Sb. The results demonstrated that as the reaction temperature increased, the molecular velocity and the frequency of effective collisions also increased, thereby enhancing the HGR (Fig. 9c).

At 30 °C, the highest HGR observed was 5618 mL· mlmin−1g−1 for the Co–Sb catalyst and 5360 mlmin−1g−1 for the Co catalyst. At 60 °C, the HGR increased significantly to 18721 mlmin−1g−1 and 17686 mlmin−1g−1 for Co–Sb and Co catalysts, respectively (Fig. 9c). Additionally, the total reaction time for the Co–Sb catalyst decreased substantially from 5 min at 30 °C to just 1.5 min at 60 °C, indicating a strong positive correlation between reaction temperature and NaBH4 hydrolysis efficiency. The observed improvement in catalytic performance with increasing temperature highlights the critical role of active sites in facilitating the hydrolysis reaction. To further characterize the kinetics of hydrogen production, nth-order reaction kinetic models (Equation (4)) were applied to both catalysts. The reaction order (n) was determined to be 0.1 for the Co–Sb catalyst and 0.2 for the Co catalyst. Sb doping improves the performance of Co catalyst because the electronic properties and surface modifications of Sb create more active sites and increase the catalytic efficiency. This effect is manifested by the lower value of n, because the presence of Sb optimizes the reaction rate and reduces the sensitivity of the catalyst to NaBH4 concentration, making the Co–Sb catalyst more stable and effective.

The resulting linear regression coefficients (R2 ) were 0.997 for the Co–Sb catalyst and 0.996 for the Co catalyst, confirming that the hydrolysis process follows an nth-order kinetic model. This suggests that the reaction rate is independent of the NaBH4 concentration, in agreement with the findings reported in the literature [57]. Furthermore, the activation energy (Ea) was determined using Equation (5). Based on the slope of ln(k) versus 1/T (Fig. 9d), the activation energies for the Co–Sb and Co catalysts were calculated as 30.9 kJ mol-1 and 35.5 kJ mol-1, respectively. The lower activation energy of the Co–Sb catalyst, comparable to or even lower than that of other reported cobalt-based catalysts, indicates its superior intrinsic catalytic activity. A comparison of the catalytic performance of the Co–Sb catalyst with other cobalt-based catalysts is presented in Table 2 .

The relatively lower activation energy of the Co–Sb catalyst compared to many other catalysts reported in the literature highlights its superior catalytic performance for the hydrolysis of NaBH4. This enhanced catalytic efficiency can be attributed to the unique properties of Co–Sb nanoparticles, particularly their synthesis via green synthesis methods. The reduced activation energy of both Co and Co–Sb catalysts, in comparison to other Co-based catalysts, is likely a result of the environmentally friendly synthesis approach, which may lead to improved surface characteristics and higher active site availability.

The stability of the Co–Sb catalyst was evaluated by subjecting it to repeated hydrolysis cycles. After each cycle, the catalyst was recovered using a magnet, washed thoroughly with deionized water, and dried at 100 °C before being reused in the subsequent cycle. Fig. 10 presents the time-dependent hydrogen generation profile of the Co–Sb catalyst over five consecutive cycles, providing insight into its durability and reusability.

Analysis of the data reveals a gradual decline in the hydrogen generation rate across consecutive cycles, a common phenomenon observed in catalytic processes. While the total hydrogen yield remained consistent over the first three cycles, variations in the hydrogen generation rate were noted. Specifically, the HGR was measured at 5618 mlmin−1g−1in the first cycle, decreasing to 4354 mlmin−1g−1 in the second cycle, 2554 mlmin−1g−1 in the third cycle, 1722 mlmin−1g−1 in the fourth cycle, and 1134 mlmin−1g−1 in the fifth cycle. The observed decline in catalytic performance can be attributed to several factors, including the accumulation of by-products such as sodium metaborate, the formation of an oxide layer on the catalyst surface, and the dissolution of active sites in sodium borohydride and alkaline solutions [22,68]. After the fifth cycle, a significant reduction in catalytic activity was detected, as evidenced by the noticeable decrease in hydrogen generation. Oxidation and passive layer formation on the catalyst surface are among the primary causes of stability loss. Although Sb doping partially mitigates excessive oxidation of Co, the accumulation of oxide and hydroxide layers over repeated cycles can lead to the deactivation of active sites, thereby reducing catalytic efficiency. Additionally, sodium metaborate, formed as a byproduct of hydrolysis, can precipitate and block active regions, further decreasing catalyst performance. The dissolution or leaching of Sb and Co atoms can also compromise the structural integrity of the catalyst, leading to a decline in its long-term activity. Over time, the electronic interactions facilitated by Sb doping may weaken, and the loss of Sb atoms could result in the catalyst exhibiting performance similar to that of undoped Co. Furthermore, reproducibility tests have shown that the catalyst experiences a gradual mass loss after each washing cycle. This reduction in catalyst mass leads to a decrease in the amount of active catalytic material, prolonging the hydrolysis process and ultimately reducing the HGR. Nevertheless, these findings suggest that the Co–Sb catalyst exhibits good stability and can be effectively utilized in multiple cycles for the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride.

In studies investigating the sodium borohydride hydrolysis reaction, the optimal operating conditions for the Co–Sb NP catalyst were identified. Within this framework, the hydrogen gas production performance of the 1 wt% Sb-doped Co–Sb NP catalyst at a solution temperature of 30 °C was assessed through proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cell tests. The current-voltage data obtained from these tests were transformed into a polarization curve to evaluate the overall system performance, as illustrated in Fig. 11 a. Additionally, Fig. 11b presents the operating time graph of the PEM fuel cell under a constant resistance of 10 Ω, demonstrating a total gas output of 690 ml over 5 min during the hydrolysis of sodium borohydride.

These findings highlight that the maximum power density obtained in the system, when utilizing the 1 wt% Sb-doped Co–Sb NP catalyst in the PEM fuel cell, reached 137.3 mW/cm2. This result underscores the significant catalytic efficiency of the Sb-doped Co–Sb nanoparticles in facilitating hydrogen generation through sodium borohydride hydrolysis. The enhanced performance can be attributed to the synergistic effects of Sb doping, which may contribute to improved active site availability, increased charge transfer efficiency, and superior catalytic stability. Additionally, the catalyst exhibited a sustained and efficient hydrogen production rate, ensuring a stable gas supply to the PEM fuel cell. This stable hydrogen flow played a crucial role in maintaining the fuel cell's performance over time. These results collectively suggest that the Sb doped Co–Sb NP catalyst not only improves the efficiency of sodium borohydride hydrolysis but also holds substantial promise for enhancing the operational efficiency, stability, and overall viability of PEM fuel cells in hydrogen energy applications.

The catalytic performance of Co–Sb nanoparticles in sodium borohydride hydrolysis is based on the synergistic interactions between Co and Sb at the atomic and molecular levels. Doping Co nanoparticles with transition metals such as Ni, Cu, Cr, Fe, or W modifies the electronic structure and surface properties of Co, thereby improving the reaction mechanism in various aspects [69,70]. The integration of Sb atoms into the Co lattice alters the d-band center of Co, optimizing the binding energy of hydrogen intermediates and consequently enhancing catalytic efficiency [71]. Based on the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model, a possible mechanism for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 has been proposed [72]. In the proposed mechanism, NaBH4 and H2O molecules adsorb on the surface and react to form B(OH)4 − and H2. Considering this context, an acceptable mechanism for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 by the Co–Sb NPs catalyst is schematically presented in Fig. 12 . Initially, NaBH4 and H2O molecules adsorb on the surface of the catalyst and become active. The hydride (H−) in BH4 − is then transferred and reacts with H2O to produce H2 and OH−. After OH− interacts with BH4 − molecule, BH3OH ion is formed. This process continues until all the H2 in BH3OH ion is replaced by OH−, and B(OH)4 − ion is formed. Four H2 molecules are released with complete conversion of BH4− ion to B(OH)4− ion to complete the cycle. After that, the next NaBH4 molecule can be hydrolyzed using a catalyst. Numerous studies in the literature support a similar reaction mechanism [73–75].

In this study, Co–Sb nanoparticles synthesized via green synthesis were investigated as catalysts for sodium borohydride hydrolysis. The results showed that 1 wt% Sb doping enhanced catalytic performance, while higher Sb contents reduced efficiency by blocking active sites. The 1 wt% Sb doped Co–Sb catalyst exhibited the highest hydrogen generation rate, confirming the synergistic effect between Co and Sb. The impact of NaOH and NaBH4 concentrations, reaction temperature, and catalyst reusability was examined. The hydrogen generation rate increased with temperature, reaching 18721 mlmin−1g−1 at 60 °C. Kinetic studies showed that the hydrolysis reaction followed an nth-order kinetic model, with activation energies of 30.9 kJ mol−1 for Co–Sb and 35.5 kJ mol−1 for Co. Stability tests indicated a gradual decline in activity due to by-product formation and active site degradation, yet the Co–Sb catalyst remained effective for multiple cycles. Additionally, 1 wt% Sb-doped Co–Sb NPs were tested in a PEM fuel cell, achieving a maximum power density of 137.3 mW/cm2. This result highlights the catalyst's efficiency in hydrogen generation and its suitability for fuel cell applications. Overall, Co–Sb nanoparticles exhibit excellent catalytic activity, stability, and potential for clean energy applications. The green synthesis method enhances performance while ensuring environmental sustainability. These findings suggest that Co–Sb catalysts, particularly with Sb doping, are promising candidates for future hydrogen storage and fuel cell technologies.
Arzu Ekinci: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Ömer Şahin: Methodology. Sevnur Turgut: Methodology, Investigation. Orhan Baytar: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Manuscript is not funded by any institution or person. Therefore no one will claim rights.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Siirt University Scientific Research Project Coordination for their support, Project No 2024-SİÜMÜH-003.
